behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Top choice: Integration. Runner-up: System. Rejection of System: behavior says "signature does not match its body" and "is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded"; this failure boundary is HTTP handler + raw-body extraction + signature verifier + queue handoff, which can be exercised in-process without real network/OS. Rejection-gate evidence for not choosing Unit: the phrase "raw request body" means module handoff bugs (parser bytes vs verifier bytes) are the real risk. Integration-brake checks: (a) real OS/network/subprocess needed? No. (b) real AI/ML output needed? No. (c) multiple agentic steps? No. (d) LLM-governed orchestration? No.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real app stack in-process (test HTTP server object), send a request with a deliberately bad `X-Signature-256` over a raw byte payload, assert `401`, and assert queue `enqueue` is never invoked / no queue record exists.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: Top choice: Integration. Runner-up: System. Rejection of System: behavior is ordering between local modules: "written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment"; this can be proven with in-process server + real persistent queue adapter (e.g., local DB/filesystem) and instrumentation of write completion vs response send. Rejection-gate evidence for not choosing Unit: ordering crosses handler and persistence boundary, not one function. Integration-brake: (a) No required real network/OS primitive beyond local in-process I/O. (b) No AI/ML output dependency. (c) No multi-agentic chain. (d) No LLM orchestration.
behaviors.test_description: Use the real queue persistence implementation (not mocked durability) in a test environment, inject a controllable delay at write commit boundary, send webhook, and assert response is not emitted until commit callback resolves; then assert `200` after durable write marker exists.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Top choice: Integration. Runner-up: System. Rejection of System: behavior text is about decoupling ack from processing: "returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing"; can be caught with in-process app where processor promise is intentionally blocked while response timing is observed. Rejection-gate evidence for not choosing Unit: requires interaction among handler, queue dispatch, and processor lifecycle. Integration-brake: (a) No real network/subprocess required. (b) No AI dependency. (c) No multi-agentic chain. (d) No LLM orchestration.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler + queue + worker in test; make worker block on a latch for several seconds, send valid webhook, assert response status is returned before latch release and event remains pending for async processing.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: Top choice: System. Runner-up: Integration. Rejection of Integration: behavior explicitly requires "within 3 seconds wall-clock time" under "slow and concurrent" conditions; mocked clocks/in-process-only fakes can hide scheduler/event-loop/backpressure effects that appear under real server concurrency. Rejection-gate keeps higher level because no concrete text disqualifies System; timing+concurrency is itself real infra behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Run the service as a real process, pre-load queue with slow-running jobs, then issue concurrent valid webhooks via real HTTP client; measure end-to-end wall-clock latency and assert each response <= 3s while backlog is being processed.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Top choice: Integration. Runner-up: System. Rejection of System: dedup is persistence/state handoff behavior from handler to queue store: "ID was already received is not written ... a second time"; reproducible with in-process modules plus real local persistence/unique constraint and concurrent requests. Rejection-gate evidence for not choosing Unit: duplicate prevention usually fails at module/state boundary (idempotency key lookup/write race), not single-function logic. Integration-brake: (a) No mandatory real network/subprocess. (b) No AI/ML dependency. (c) No multi-agentic steps. (d) No LLM orchestration.
behaviors.test_description: Use real dedup store/queue adapter, send same event ID twice (including near-simultaneous requests), assert only one persisted queue record and second request returns expected idempotent outcome without a second enqueue.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Top choice: Integration. Runner-up: Unit. Rejection of Unit: behavior spans queue error contract to HTTP mapping: "queue reports it is full" -> "handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header"; this is inter-module translation, not pure local logic. Rejection-gate for higher levels: no text requiring real OS/network/subprocess beyond in-process HTTP stack, and no AI dependency. Integration-brake: (a) No. (b) No. (c) No. (d) No.
behaviors.test_description: Run handler with queue module configured to return a typed `QueueFull` condition, send valid webhook, assert HTTP `503` and correct `Retry-After` header value/format, and verify no success ack path was taken.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: Top choice: Integration. Runner-up: System. Rejection of System: behavior is queue storage semantics: "persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit"; this requires real queue implementation with local persistence, but not necessarily subprocess/real network. Rejection-gate evidence for not choosing Unit: requires persistence behavior over multiple writes and configuration boundary, not one function. Integration-brake: (a) No required external OS/network primitive beyond local persistence API. (b) No AI dependency. (c) No multi-agentic chain. (d) No LLM orchestration.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue backend with a small fixed capacity, enqueue until limit, then attempt one more enqueue and assert explicit rejection/error and unchanged persisted depth.
